name: Syria
tagline: From Ebla and Ugarit's first alphabet to the Umayyad caliphate and a republic remade in 2024.
summary: Syria is one of the cradles of urban civilisation. In the third and second millennia BCE the city-kingdoms of Ebla, Mari and Ugarit flourished here, and at Ugarit scribes devised one of the world's earliest alphabets. Damascus and Aleppo are among the oldest continuously inhabited cities on earth; the desert caravan city of Palmyra rose under Queen Zenobia, and after the Muslim conquest Damascus became capital of the Umayyad Caliphate (661–750), the first great Islamic empire. Conquered in turn by Crusaders, Ayyubids under Saladin, Mamluks and Ottomans, Syria won independence from France on 17 April 1946; after decades of unstable republics, union with Egypt and Ba'athist rule under Hafez and then Bashar al-Assad, a 2011 uprising spiralled into civil war, and the Assad government fell in December 2024.

founding.label: Independence of the Syrian Republic — evacuation of French troops
founding.detail: Modern Syria emerged from the French Mandate established after the First World War. Although a republic was proclaimed and won international recognition in 1944, full sovereignty came only when the last French soldiers left Syrian soil on 17 April 1946 — still commemorated as Evacuation Day, the national founding moment. The state that became independent in 1946 stands at the end of an immensely long history reaching back through Ottoman, Mamluk, Ayyubid, Crusader, Umayyad, Roman and Bronze Age phases to the city-kingdoms of Ebla, Mari and Ugarit.

quickFacts.label: Independence (Evacuation Day)
quickFacts.value: 17 April 1946, end of the French Mandate

quickFacts.label: Ancient capitals
quickFacts.value: Ebla, Mari, Ugarit and Aram-Damascus in the Bronze and Iron Ages

quickFacts.label: Umayyad capital
quickFacts.value: Damascus, seat of the first Islamic dynasty, 661–750 CE

quickFacts.label: Oldest cities
quickFacts.value: Damascus and Aleppo, among the world's oldest continuously inhabited cities (UNESCO)

quickFacts.label: Government change
quickFacts.value: Assad government fell on 8 December 2024 after 13 years of civil war

eras.title: Ebla, Mari, Ugarit and the First Alphabet
eras.standfirst: In the Bronze Age, the city-kingdoms of Ebla, Mari and Ugarit made Syria one of the cradles of writing, trade and statecraft, and at Ugarit scribes created one of the world's earliest alphabets.
body: Long before there was a country called Syria, its plains and river valleys cradled some of the earliest cities on earth. At Ebla, southwest of Aleppo, a powerful kingdom dominated northern Syria around 2600–2240 BCE, trading and corresponding with states as distant as Egypt and Sumer. Its rediscovery in the 1960s and 1970s, when an Italian team uncovered thousands of clay tablets, opened a window onto a literate Bronze Age society whose archives recorded administration, treaties and tribute in cuneiform.

On the Euphrates to the southeast stood Mari, one of the earliest planned cities and a great commercial rival to Ebla. Excavations begun in 1933 revealed the vast palace of King Zimri-Lim, with nearly 300 rooms and archives of diplomatic letters that illuminate the politics of the early second millennium BCE, until Hammurabi of Babylon captured and destroyed the city in the eighteenth century BCE. Mari's correspondence, exchanged with kings across Mesopotamia, ranks among the richest written sources of the age.

On the Mediterranean coast, the merchant city of Ugarit reached its height in the fourteenth and thirteenth centuries BCE. There, around 1400 BCE, scribes developed a cuneiform alphabet of some thirty signs — among the earliest alphabetic writing systems known — a landmark in the long road from pictographs to the alphabets later used across the Mediterranean. The Bronze Age world of these cities collapsed around 1200 BCE amid wider upheavals across the eastern Mediterranean.

events.title: The kingdom of Ebla
events.summary: Ebla dominates northern Syria, trading and corresponding with Egypt, Sumer and Mesopotamia; its cuneiform royal archives, rediscovered in the 20th century, reveal a literate Bronze Age state.

events.title: Mari and the palace of Zimri-Lim
events.summary: The Euphrates city of Mari flourishes as a planned trading capital, its palace archives preserving thousands of diplomatic letters, until Hammurabi of Babylon destroys it in the 18th century BCE.

events.title: The Ugaritic alphabet
events.summary: At the coastal city of Ugarit, scribes devise a cuneiform alphabet of about 30 signs — one of the earliest alphabetic writing systems in history, ancestral in spirit to later Semitic and Mediterranean alphabets.

eras.title: Aramaeans, Rome and the Glory of Palmyra
eras.standfirst: After the Bronze Age came the Aramaeans and their enduring language, then Greek and Roman rule, the rise of early Christianity, and the desert empire of Palmyra under Queen Zenobia.
body: Into the Iron Age moved the Aramaeans, a Semitic people whose tribes settled across northern Syria between the eleventh and eighth centuries BCE. They made Damascus the centre of a powerful kingdom, Aram-Damascus, known from Assyrian and biblical records. Their greatest legacy was Aramaic, a language that spread far beyond Syria to become the lingua franca of the whole Near East for over a thousand years, until the coming of Islam. The Assyrians, Babylonians and then the Persians successively absorbed these lands.

After Alexander the Great, Syria became the heartland of the Hellenistic Seleucid kingdom, its cities thoroughly Greek in culture, until Pompey annexed Syria to Rome in 64 BCE. Under Roman rule Damascus and Antioch flourished, and Syria became a major centre of early Christianity: according to the New Testament, Paul the Apostle was converted on the road to Damascus, and Antioch was where followers were first called Christians. The province grew rich on the trade linking the Mediterranean with Mesopotamia, Persia and beyond.

Astride the desert caravan routes rose Palmyra, an oasis city that married Graeco-Roman, Persian and local styles in monumental colonnades and temples. In the third century CE its queen Zenobia, ruling as regent for her young son, broke from Rome, seizing Egypt and much of Asia Minor before the emperor Aurelian crushed her armies and captured Palmyra in 272 CE. The ruins of Palmyra, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, remain among the most celebrated monuments of the ancient world.

events.title: The Aramaeans and Aram-Damascus
events.summary: Aramaean tribes settle Syria and make Damascus the capital of a powerful kingdom; their Aramaic language becomes the lingua franca of the Near East for over a millennium.

events.title: Pompey annexes Syria to Rome
events.summary: The Roman general Pompey ends Seleucid rule and makes Syria a Roman province; its cities prosper and Syria becomes an early heartland of Christianity, with Paul converted on the road to Damascus.

events.title: Zenobia, Palmyra and the war with Rome
events.summary: Queen Zenobia of Palmyra breaks from Rome and conquers Egypt and Asia Minor, before the emperor Aurelian defeats her armies and captures the city; Palmyra's ruins are today a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

figures.name: Zenobia
figures.blurb: Regent and queen of the caravan city of Palmyra who challenged Rome, seizing Egypt and much of Asia Minor before being defeated and captured by the emperor Aurelian. She became an enduring symbol of Syrian and ancient female sovereignty.

pullquote.text: Palmyra was one of the most important cultural centres of the ancient world.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO World Heritage Centre, on the Site of Palmyra

eras.title: Islam and the Umayyad Caliphate of Damascus
eras.standfirst: After the Byzantine centuries, the Muslim conquest of the 630s made Damascus the capital of the Umayyad Caliphate, the first great Islamic empire, stretching from Spain to Central Asia.
body: With the division of the Roman world, Syria fell to the Byzantine (East Roman) Empire, a prosperous Christian province whose churches and monasteries dotted the landscape. That order was transformed in the 630s CE when Arab Muslim armies, advancing out of Arabia, defeated the Byzantines and took Damascus, Aleppo and the rest of Syria within a few short years. Much of the population, long Aramaic- and Greek-speaking, gradually adopted Arabic and Islam, though substantial Christian and other communities endured.

In 661 CE Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan, governor of Syria, founded the Umayyad dynasty and made Damascus the capital of the Islamic Caliphate. For nearly a century Syria was the centre of an empire that stretched from Spain and North Africa to Central Asia and the Indus — the first great Muslim dynasty. The Umayyads built one of Islam's earliest and grandest monuments, the Great Mosque of Damascus, raised under Caliph al-Walid between 705 and 715 on a site sacred successively to Roman, Christian and Muslim worship.

The Umayyad century ended in 750 CE when the Abbasid revolution overthrew the dynasty and moved the caliphate's centre to the new city of Baghdad, demoting Syria to a province. Over the following centuries Syria passed under a succession of regional powers and dynasties, and from 1098–1099 the armies of the First Crusade swept down the Levantine coast, establishing Crusader states and capturing Jerusalem, opening two centuries of intermittent holy war on Syrian soil.

events.title: The Muslim conquest of Syria
events.summary: Arab Muslim armies defeat the Byzantines and take Damascus and Aleppo within a few years; over time Arabic and Islam spread, though Christian and other communities remain.

events.title: Damascus, capital of the Umayyad Caliphate
events.summary: Muawiya founds the Umayyad dynasty and makes Damascus the capital of the first great Islamic empire, ruling from Spain to Central Asia until the Abbasid revolution of 750.

events.title: The Great Mosque of Damascus
events.summary: Under Caliph al-Walid the Umayyads build the Great (Umayyad) Mosque of Damascus, one of the oldest and grandest mosques in the world, on a site holy to Roman, Christian and Muslim worship.

events.title: The First Crusade reaches Syria
events.summary: Crusader armies seize cities along the Levantine coast and capture Jerusalem in 1099, founding Crusader states and beginning two centuries of intermittent warfare across Syria.

pullquote.text: In 661 the Umayyad dynasty chose Damascus to be the capital of the Muslim world.
pullquote.attribution: On the founding of the Umayyad Caliphate

eras.title: Saladin, the Mamluks and Ottoman Rule
eras.standfirst: Saladin's Ayyubids drove back the Crusaders and recaptured Jerusalem; the Mamluks then ruled Syria until the Ottomans absorbed it in 1516, governing for four centuries until the First World War.
body: The Crusader presence in the Levant was challenged above all by Saladin (Salah al-Din), a Kurdish commander who united Egypt and Syria under the Ayyubid dynasty he founded in the late twelfth century. In 1187 he decisively defeated the Crusaders and recaptured Jerusalem, ending nearly nine decades of Frankish rule there, and he became one of the most celebrated figures of the medieval Islamic world. His tomb stands beside the Great Mosque of Damascus.

The Ayyubids gave way in 1250 to the Mamluks, a military caste of former slave-soldiers based in Egypt who ruled Syria as well. The Mamluks halted the Mongol advance into Syria and governed its cities for more than two and a half centuries. Aleppo and Damascus thrived as centres of trade, scholarship and craft, their citadels, mosques and covered markets giving the old cities much of the form preserved in their UNESCO-listed historic cores.

In 1516 the Ottoman sultan Selim I defeated the Mamluks and absorbed Syria into the Ottoman Empire, which would rule for four hundred years. As Ottoman provinces, Damascus and Aleppo remained great commercial and religious hubs, Damascus serving as a gathering point for the annual pilgrimage caravan to Mecca. Ottoman rule lasted until the First World War, when the Arab Revolt and British forces drove the Ottomans from Syria in 1918, ending four centuries of Turkish dominion.

events.title: Saladin recaptures Jerusalem
events.summary: Saladin, founder of the Ayyubid dynasty that united Egypt and Syria, defeats the Crusaders and retakes Jerusalem, ending nearly nine decades of Frankish rule and becoming a legendary figure of the age.

events.title: Mamluk rule over Syria
events.summary: The Mamluks supplant the Ayyubids and govern Syria from Egypt for over two centuries, halting the Mongols and presiding over a flourishing of trade and architecture in Damascus and Aleppo.

events.title: Ottoman conquest of Syria
events.summary: Sultan Selim I defeats the Mamluks and incorporates Syria into the Ottoman Empire, beginning four centuries of Ottoman rule over Damascus, Aleppo and the Syrian lands.

events.title: End of Ottoman rule
events.summary: During the First World War the Arab Revolt and Allied forces drive the Ottomans from Syria, ending four hundred years of Ottoman dominion and opening the way to French and British arrangements for the region.

figures.name: Saladin (Salah al-Din)
figures.blurb: A Kurdish general who united Egypt and Syria, founded the Ayyubid dynasty and recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187. Renowned in both Muslim and Christian traditions for his statesmanship and chivalry, he is buried beside the Great Mosque of Damascus.

eras.title: The French Mandate and Independence
eras.standfirst: A brief Arab kingdom in 1920 gave way to French Mandate rule; Syria won independence in 1946, then endured coups, a short-lived union with Egypt, and the Ba'athist seizure of power.
body: With the Ottomans gone, Arab nationalists proclaimed an independent Arab Kingdom of Syria in Damascus in March 1920 under Faisal ibn Husayn of the Hashemite family. It lasted only months: the League of Nations assigned Syria to France as a mandate, and French troops defeated the kingdom's outnumbered forces at the Battle of Maysalun in July 1920, where the defence minister Yusuf al-Azma was killed. France then governed Syria, dividing and ruling it and suppressing a major revolt in 1925–27, while nationalist demands for independence steadily grew.

During the Second World War, British and Free French forces took Syria from the Vichy administration, and independence was promised. A republic was proclaimed and won international recognition in 1944, but full sovereignty came only when the last French soldiers withdrew on 17 April 1946 — celebrated ever since as Evacuation Day. The young republic, however, proved deeply unstable, shaken by a series of military coups beginning in 1949 amid the shock of the 1948 Arab-Israeli war and Cold War rivalries.

In February 1958 Syria merged with Gamal Abdel Nasser's Egypt to form the United Arab Republic, a pan-Arab union driven partly by Syria's Ba'ath Party. Disillusion with Egyptian domination ended the union in 1961, and after further turmoil the Ba'ath Party seized power in a coup in March 1963. A faction-ridden decade of Ba'athist rule followed until, in November 1970, the air-force commander Hafez al-Assad took control in a coup he called the Corrective Movement, beginning a rule that would last three decades.

events.title: Arab Kingdom of Syria and the French Mandate
events.summary: An independent Arab Kingdom of Syria under Faisal is proclaimed in Damascus, but French forces defeat it at Maysalun in July 1920, and the League of Nations assigns Syria to France as a mandate.

events.title: Independence — evacuation of French troops
events.summary: After international recognition in 1944, the last French soldiers leave Syria on 17 April 1946, marking full independence — still commemorated as Evacuation Day.

events.title: Union with Egypt — the United Arab Republic
events.summary: Syria merges with Nasser's Egypt to form the United Arab Republic in 1958, but resentment of Egyptian dominance dissolves the union in a 1961 coup.

events.title: Hafez al-Assad seizes power
events.summary: After the Ba'ath Party's 1963 coup and years of factional struggle, defence minister Hafez al-Assad takes control in November 1970, founding a family rule that would endure for over half a century.

figures.name: Yusuf al-Azma
figures.role: Defence minister of the Arab Kingdom of Syria
figures.blurb: Minister of war who, despite the odds, led Syrian forces against the advancing French army at the Battle of Maysalun in July 1920 and was killed in the fighting. He is remembered in Syria as a national symbol of resistance to colonial rule.

eras.title: The Assad Era, the Civil War and the Fall of 2024
eras.standfirst: Hafez and then Bashar al-Assad ruled Syria for more than five decades; a 2011 uprising became a devastating civil war, and in December 2024 the Assad government fell.
body: Hafez al-Assad governed Syria from 1970 until his death in 2000, building a centralised, single-party state dominated by the Ba'ath Party and the security services. His rule was marked by Cold War alignment with the Soviet Union, intervention in Lebanon, confrontation with Israel, and the harsh suppression of dissent — most notoriously the 1982 crackdown in Hama. On his death he was succeeded by his son Bashar al-Assad, who initially raised hopes of reform but maintained his father's authoritarian system.

In 2011, amid the wider Arab Spring, protests against the government spread across Syria. The state's violent response, and the rise of armed opposition groups, drew the country into a civil war of extreme complexity, drawing in regional and global powers, jihadist movements including the Islamic State, and Kurdish-led forces. The conflict is reported to have been among the deadliest of the century: the UN human rights office (OHCHR) estimated some 306,887 civilians killed over the first decade, while the UN refugee agency reports that the war displaced more than 13 million people, including over 6 million refugees abroad. These figures are estimates from authoritative bodies and remain contested.

After years of military stalemate, a rebel offensive led by the group Hay'at Tahrir al-Sham advanced swiftly in late 2024, and on 8 December 2024 the Assad government fell as Bashar al-Assad fled the country, ending more than five decades of family rule. An interim administration was established under Ahmed al-Sharaa, and the country entered an uncertain transition. Across all its upheavals, Syria's deep heritage — Damascus and Aleppo among the world's oldest cities, Palmyra, and the other UNESCO sites scarred by the war — endures as a record of one of humanity's oldest civilisations.

events.title: Bashar al-Assad succeeds his father
events.summary: On Hafez al-Assad's death, his son Bashar becomes president; early hopes of reform give way to a continuation of the authoritarian, single-party system.

events.title: Uprising and the start of the civil war
events.summary: Arab Spring protests across Syria meet a violent state response; armed opposition emerges and the country descends into a civil war drawing in regional and global powers.

events.title: Human cost of the war
events.summary: The UN human rights office estimates about 306,887 civilians killed over the war's first decade, while the UN refugee agency reports more than 13 million people displaced — figures cited as estimates from authoritative bodies.

events.title: The Assad government falls
events.summary: A rebel offensive led by Hay'at Tahrir al-Sham takes Damascus on 8 December 2024; Bashar al-Assad flees the country, ending over five decades of Assad family rule, and an interim government is formed.

figures.name: Hafez al-Assad
figures.blurb: An air-force commander who seized power in 1970 and ruled Syria for three decades, building a centralised one-party state aligned with the Soviet Union and dominated by the security services. He was succeeded on his death by his son Bashar.

figures.name: Bashar al-Assad
figures.blurb: Successor to his father Hafez, whose response to the 2011 uprising led to a civil war that devastated Syria. His government fell in December 2024 when he fled the country amid a rebel offensive, ending the Assad family's long rule.

pullquote.text: The UN Human Rights Office estimates more than 306,000 civilians were killed over 10 years in the Syria conflict.
pullquote.attribution: Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights, 2022

figures.name: Zenobia
figures.blurb: Queen of the Syrian caravan city of Palmyra who defied Rome, conquering Egypt and much of Asia Minor before her defeat by the emperor Aurelian. A lasting symbol of ancient Syrian power.

figures.name: Saladin (Salah al-Din)
figures.blurb: The Kurdish general who united Egypt and Syria and retook Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187, celebrated across cultures for his statesmanship and buried beside the Great Mosque of Damascus.

figures.name: Hafez al-Assad
figures.blurb: Founder of the Assad family's rule, who seized power in 1970 and built a centralised single-party state. His son Bashar succeeded him in 2000, and the dynasty's rule ended in December 2024.
